general_concerns: Believes insomnia is purely a chemical imbalance and that only strong sleeping pills can fix it, not therapy or sleep habits.
Thinks getting less than 8 hours every night is dangerous and will inevitably cause dementia or a heart attack soon.
Assumes if a medication works (e.g., pentobarbital used in the past), it must be safe to use long-term as long as the dose is 'small.'
Believes alcohol or antihistamines are safer than prescription sleep medications because they are over-the-counter or socially common.
Thinks snoring or pauses in breathing are unrelated to insomnia, so screening for sleep apnea is a 'waste of time.'
Believes checking the clock and staying in bed longer will help 'catch up' on sleep, even if awake for hours.
economic_concerns: Worries that non-medication treatments (CBT-I, sleep studies) are expensive and not covered, so insists on the cheapest pill-based solution.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old HR manager. You’re tired, frustrated, and want a quick, cheap pill. Start vague and a bit evasive: say you “can’t sleep” and it’s ruining your life. Volunteer only: lying awake for hours, frequent awakenings, daytime exhaustion and poor focus, worsening for months. If asked, add: racing thoughts at night, early waking, irritability, more coffee, OTC sleep aids not working, weekend naps, phone use in bed.

Withhold unless directly and specifically asked: occasional snoring and waking up gasping, jumpy legs/urge to move, weight gain, nighttime heartburn, little daylight/exercise.

Be resistant/defensive if substances are asked: minimize cannabis gummies, avoid details about 2–3 drinks most nights, and only admit using a friend’s prescription sleep pill if pressed. Mention “panic-y” nighttime feelings only if asked about anxiety.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid or ADHD, trust sleep tracker showing low deep sleep, ask about magnesium.

If clinician is empathetic, soften briefly, but return to insisting on a brand-name sleep medication and concern about CBT-I/sleep study cost and missing work.

Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: Generalized anxiety disorder with rumination at night
SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline)
Iron deficiency (low ferritin) contributing to restless legs symptoms and sleep fragmentation
Possible perimenopausal transition with intermittent night sweats (sleep disruption)
Caffeine use in late afternoon (self-reported 2-3 cups/day)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk due to BMI and reported snoring (needs screening)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)
acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia due to medication/substance (barbiturate-related sleep disturbance)
Insomnia secondary to medical condition (e.g., hyperthyroidism) pending labs
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, irritability, impaired concentration, reduced performance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week and for at least 3 months (or specify acute if <3 months)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep are present
Not better explained by another sleep disorder (e.g., OSA, RLS), substance use, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition
Review of medications/substances includes current/recent pentobarbital use (tolerance, rebound insomnia, dependence risk)

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no biochemical hyperthyroidism driving insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no uncontrolled diabetes contributing to nocturia/sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (≥50 ng/mL); if low, would support restless legs syndrome contribution
expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: Within reference range (no hyponatremia-related neurocognitive symptoms)

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (consider for sleep-onset difficulty)
Ramelteon 8 mg PO nightly at bedtime (alternative to melatonin for sleep-onset insomnia)
Doxepin 3-6 mg PO nightly at bedtime (for sleep-maintenance insomnia; avoid higher antidepressant doses)
Suvorexant 10 mg PO nightly at bedtime (if needed; use lowest effective dose; caution next-day somnolence)
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit time in bed, avoid naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, limit screens/bright light 1-2 hours before bed
Relaxation training (progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, mindfulness)
Address contributing factors: pain, nocturia, GERD, anxiety/depression screening and management
If concern for sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), arrange sleep study rather than sedative escalation
Avoid/stop pentobarbital for insomnia; if currently using regularly, plan supervised taper to prevent withdrawal and rebound insomnia
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary response to CBT-I/med changes; earlier (within 3-7 days) if starting a new sedating medication or if withdrawal risk from pentobarbital taper is present.

must_include: Define insomnia as difficulty falling asleep/ staying asleep/ early awakening with daytime impairment
Clarify chronic vs short-term timeframe and triggers
Explain that medical causes (thyroid disease, iron deficiency/RLS, glycemic issues) are being screened with labs
Discuss that barbiturates (pentobarbital) are not recommended for insomnia due to tolerance, dependence, overdose and rebound insomnia
CBT-I is first-line and has the best long-term benefit
If using medication, use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration and avoid combining sedatives
Review chosen medication’s main risks: next-day sedation, falls, impaired driving, complex sleep behaviors (if applicable), and need to avoid alcohol/other CNS depressants
If pentobarbital is being used, explain need for supervised taper and withdrawal warning signs (anxiety, tremor, agitation, seizures)
Elicit patient goals and prior sleep treatments
Assess safety: suicidal ideation, substance use, driving/occupational hazards, fall risk
Discuss expectations: gradual improvement over weeks with CBT-I; track progress with sleep diary
Provide specific sleep schedule plan (fixed wake time; limit time in bed to consolidate sleep)
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine guidance and screen/light exposure timing
When to seek urgent care: severe withdrawal symptoms, confusion, breathing problems, or severe daytime sleepiness affecting safety

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia complaint details (onset/duration, sleep pattern, daytime impairment), triggers, sleep environment, and prior treatments; screen for red flags and contributors (mood, OSA symptoms, RLS, pain, meds/substances).

Assess safety/risks relevant to insomnia management: suicidal ideation, substance use disorder, driving/occupational hazards, fall risk, and current sedative/hypnotic use including any barbiturate (e.g., pentobarbital) and dose/duration.
Order screening labs for medical contributors per context (e.g., TSH, ferritin/iron studies, glucose/A1c as appropriate) and any additional labs indicated by history.
Review lab results to support/adjust differential (thyroid disease, iron deficiency/RLS, glycemic issues) and ensure safe plan.
Make and document insomnia diagnosis (acute vs chronic) with contributing factors and comorbidities; identify if medication-induced or substance-related insomnia is likely.

Explain diagnosis (definition, chronic vs short-term) and that labs screen medical causes; explicitly counsel that barbiturates (pentobarbital) are not recommended for insomnia due to tolerance, dependence, overdose, and rebound insomnia.

Provide first-line treatment plan: CBT-I referral/components, sleep diary, stimulus control, sleep restriction with a specific schedule plan (fixed wake time; limit time in bed), and substance/light guidance.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication.
Critical safety check before prescribing; include CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, gabapentinoids, and other sedatives.

Critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, OSA/respiratory disease, history of complex sleep behaviors, SUD risk, fall risk/elderly, driving hazards) and noncritical check before ordering if relevant.

If medication is indicated after CBT-I discussion and risk assessment, prescribe the safest appropriate short-term option at lowest effective dose for shortest duration; avoid combining sedatives; avoid barbiturates. If patient is already using pentobarbital, do not renew for insomnia and instead plan supervised taper/withdrawal risk mitigation.

Explain treatment risks and precautions: next-day sedation, falls, impaired driving, complex sleep behaviors (if applicable), and avoiding alcohol/other CNS depressants; if pentobarbital use present, counsel supervised taper and withdrawal warning signs (anxiety, tremor, agitation, seizures).

Arrange follow-up to review sleep diary/CBT-I progress, reassess safety and medication effects, and review lab results; provide urgent return precautions (severe withdrawal, confusion, breathing problems, severe daytime sleepiness affecting safety).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety net.

nl_assertions: Agent defines insomnia as difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early awakening with associated daytime impairment.
Agent clarifies whether insomnia is short-term vs chronic (≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months) and explores triggers (stress, schedule changes, substances, medications).
Agent elicits patient goals and prior sleep treatments, including prior CBT-I components and medication trials.

Agent screens for key contributors/comorbidities: depression/anxiety (including suicidal ideation), OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas), RLS symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD, and substance use (alcohol, cannabis, stimulants).
Agent assesses safety risks relevant to insomnia management: driving/occupational hazards, fall risk, and concurrent sedative use.
Agent orders or justifies screening labs for medical causes consistent with the context (thyroid disease, iron deficiency/RLS, glycemic issues) and explains their purpose to the patient.
Agent communicates that CBT-I is first-line and provides a concrete plan including fixed wake time, limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep, and use of a sleep diary.
Agent provides lifestyle counseling on caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing and screen/light exposure management.
Agent explicitly states that barbiturates (including pentobarbital) are not recommended for insomnia due to tolerance, dependence, overdose risk, and rebound insomnia.
If medication is used, agent emphasizes lowest effective dose for shortest duration and avoidance of combining sedatives/CNS depressants.
Agent reviews medication-specific risks: next-day sedation, falls, impaired driving, and complex sleep behaviors when applicable, and advises avoiding alcohol/other CNS depressants.
If pentobarbital use is present, agent recommends supervised taper (not abrupt cessation) and educates on withdrawal warning signs (anxiety, tremor, agitation, seizures) and when to seek urgent care.
Before any prescribing, agent performs critical safety checks: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error.
Agent provides clear urgent-care precautions: severe withdrawal symptoms, confusion, breathing problems, or severe daytime sleepiness affecting safety.
Agent schedules follow-up to reassess symptoms, review sleep diary and lab results, and monitor any medication effects or taper progress.

general_concerns: Thinks nephritis is always caused by a urinary tract infection and that antibiotics alone will cure it.
Believes that if she drinks a lot of water and cranberry juice, the kidney inflammation will go away without further testing.
Assumes that because her urine looks normal some days, her kidneys must be recovering and follow-up labs are optional.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “My pee has been really dark and kind of foamy, and my face looks puffy in the mornings.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tea-colored urine for ~1 week, foamy urine, morning eyelid/face puffiness, ankle swelling by day’s end, more fatigue, and mention your second finger has always looked short.

If asked about urine amount, weight, headaches, blood, infections, or pain, share: peeing less despite same fluids, a few pounds rapid gain, mild pressure-like headaches, occasional pink urine, sore throat 2 weeks ago, and dull bilateral low back ache.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: a high BP reading at a pharmacy, new shortness of breath on stairs, tighter wedding ring, near-daily ibuprofen for a month, protein powder/creatine.

Be resistant about supplements/working out; worry you’ll be told to stop lifting. Admit only if asked that you used leftover antibiotics for 2 days.

Express misconceptions: you suspect UTI, exercise muscle breakdown, or high-protein diet causing foam.

If the clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly and share money worries about “extra” tests, biopsy cost, and distrust of generics.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg 1 tablet daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed up to every 6 hours (self-directed)
confounders: Recent NSAID use (can worsen kidney function and cause interstitial nephritis, complicating evaluation of glomerulonephritis)
Short distal phalanx of the 2nd finger (possible congenital brachydactyly; can distract from systemic autoimmune evaluation)
Use of estrogen-containing oral contraceptive (increases thrombotic risk; can confound edema/renal findings if renal vein thrombosis is considered)
Mild anemia of inflammation vs iron deficiency (may complicate interpretation of chronicity and systemic disease activity)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial nephritis (drug-induced or idiopathic) presenting as acute kidney injury
acceptable_alternatives: Acute glomerulonephritis (nephritic syndrome) causing acute kidney injury
Acute tubular necrosis (non-nephritic AKI) — if urinalysis/clinical context supports
Lupus nephritis — if systemic features/serologies present
required_evidence: Rising serum creatinine and/or reduced eGFR consistent with AKI
Clinical context for nephritis/renal inflammation (e.g., hematuria, proteinuria, casts) — to be confirmed by urinalysis/microscopy
Exclusion of post-renal obstruction and pre-renal causes (history, exam, volume status, renal ultrasound if indicated)
Medication/exposure review for potential AIN triggers (e.g., antibiotics, PPIs, NSAIDs) even if not initially listed
If nephritic GN suspected: supportive evidence such as hypertension/edema and abnormal urinalysis; consider serologies and complement testing

expected_lab_results.serum_creatinine: Elevated above baseline (acute rise) consistent with AKI
expected_lab_results.blood_urea_nitrogen_bun: Elevated; BUN:Cr ratio variable (often not markedly high unless pre-renal component)
expected_lab_results.eGFR_ckd_epi: Decreased (may be unreliable in rapidly changing renal function but expected to be reduced)
expected_lab_results.serum_albumin: Usually normal or mildly decreased (marked hypoalbuminemia would suggest nephrotic-range protein loss rather than isolated nephritis)
expected_lab_results.serum_sodium: Often normal; may be low if volume overload/dilutional hyponatremia
expected_lab_results.serum_potassium: Normal to elevated depending on AKI severity and acid-base status
expected_lab_results.serum_bicarbonate_co2: Low-normal to decreased (metabolic acidosis can occur with AKI)
expected_lab_results.chloride: Often normal; may be relatively increased if non-anion gap metabolic acidosis present

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride IV titrate to euvolemia (only if hypovolemic; avoid if volume overloaded)
Furosemide 20-40 mg PO/IV once, then titrate PRN for symptomatic volume overload (if present)
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO TID (if metabolic acidosis with low serum bicarbonate and no contraindication)
non_pharmacological: Stop and avoid potential nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast when possible, unnecessary antibiotics; review OTC/herbals)
Assess volume status; strict intake/output monitoring and daily weights
Renal diet as indicated (limit potassium/phosphate/sodium based on labs and volume status)
Order/obtain urinalysis with microscopy and urine protein quantification to confirm nephritic vs other AKI patterns
Consider renal ultrasound to exclude obstruction if not already excluded
Early nephrology consult if creatinine rapidly rising, suspected RPGN, significant electrolyte/acid-base derangements, or unclear diagnosis
Plan for additional workup if nephritic GN suspected (C3/C4, ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis serologies) and consider kidney biopsy when indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck BMP (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes, bicarbonate) within 24-48 hours (sooner if unstable) and arrange nephrology follow-up within 1 week or inpatient management if severe/rapidly progressive

must_include: Kidney inflammation/acute kidney injury explanation in plain language
Uncertainty and need for urine testing/imaging/possibly specialist evaluation to determine the exact cause
Warning signs that require urgent care (decreased urine, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe swelling, confusion)
Main goals: protect kidneys, correct fluid/electrolyte/acid-base problems, remove triggers
Why nephrotoxins are avoided and why medication review is essential
When dialysis might be needed (refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, volume overload, uremic symptoms) if condition worsens
Penicillin allergy implications: avoid penicillins; discuss cross-reactivity considerations if antibiotics are later needed
Discuss prognosis depends on cause and speed of treatment; many cases improve with prompt management
Acknowledge finger finding is not typical of nephritis and may represent a separate congenital/orthopedic issue; offer evaluation if patient concerned
Hydration guidance tailored to volume status (do not overdrink if swelling/heart failure suspected)
Dietary guidance: limit sodium; potassium restriction if potassium is high
Avoid OTC NSAIDs and inform clinicians before receiving contrast studies

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for nephritis/AKI: onset, urine changes (hematuria/foamy urine/oliguria), edema, dyspnea, recent infection (strep/URI), systemic symptoms (rash, arthralgias), flank pain, pregnancy status if relevant, PMH (CKD, HTN, diabetes, autoimmune), medication/OTC/herbal use (NSAIDs), recent contrast, and red flags.

Order initial workup to confirm kidney inflammation/AKI and assess severity/complications: BMP/CMP (Cr, BUN, K, bicarb), CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein quantification (UPCR/UACR), urine culture if infection possible, complements (C3/C4), ANA, anti-dsDNA/ANCA/anti-GBM as indicated, ASO/anti-DNase B if post-strep suspected, ESR/CRP; consider pregnancy test when applicable.
Order imaging when appropriate (e.g., renal ultrasound) to evaluate obstruction/size and support safe management; consider CXR if volume overload suspected.
Interpret labs/imaging for nephritic pattern (hematuria, proteinuria, RBC casts), AKI severity, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and volume overload to determine disposition and urgency.
Synthesize findings into a working diagnosis (suspected nephritis/glomerulonephritis with AKI vs alternative causes) and explicitly state uncertainty and need for further evaluation/specialist input.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Coronary artery disease due to autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia (ASCVD/CAD from inherited dyslipidemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease

required_evidence: Atherogenic lipid profile consistent with familial hypercholesterolemia (markedly elevated LDL-C and apolipoprotein B, often elevated lipoprotein(a))
Evidence of coronary artery disease (history of angina, prior MI, prior coronary revascularization, or documented coronary stenosis)
No biochemical evidence of acute myocardial infarction (high-sensitivity troponin I not showing acute rise/fall pattern)
No clinical/laboratory pattern supporting primary differentials (e.g., no features of idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension or staphylococcal pneumonia)

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated (often markedly elevated in familial hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.LDL-C: Markedly elevated (typically ≥190 mg/dL [≥4.9 mmol/L] if untreated; may remain above goal if undertreated)
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Usually normal to mildly elevated (not the primary abnormality)
expected_lab_results.Lipoprotein(a): Often elevated (a common inherited risk enhancer; may be markedly elevated)
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity troponin I: Normal or not dynamically elevated (no acute rise/fall consistent with MI)
expected_lab_results.High-sensitivity CRP: Normal to mildly elevated (may be elevated as a risk marker; not typically very high without acute infection/inflammation)

Mention misconceptions: you wondered about allergy/swelling attacks when lips/face looked puffy, and you suspect food intolerance or vitamin deficiency from online reading. correct_diagnosis.primary: Glucagonoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor secreting glucagon)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum glucagon (typically >500 pg/mL, often >1000 pg/mL) in the appropriate clinical context
Hyperglycemia and/or elevated HbA1c consistent with glucagon-mediated diabetes
Elevated chromogranin A supporting neuroendocrine tumor physiology (recognizing potential false positives)
CBC abnormalities consistent with anemia (often normocytic or mild) can support systemic effects
Exclusion of key differentials given the presentation and labs (Sandhoff disease and hereditary angioedema not supported by hyperglucagonemia/hyperglycemia pattern)

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Elevated (hyperglycemia; may be in diabetic range)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucagon (fasting): Markedly elevated (typically >500 pg/mL; often >1000 pg/mL in glucagonoma)
expected_lab_results.Chromogranin A: Elevated (supports neuroendocrine tumor; interpret with clinical context)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal or mildly low/normal (often normocytic; may vary with nutritional status)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (may be mildly elevated if inflammation/infection present)

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks
Insulin (basal-bolus regimen) SC per weight and glucose targets (titrate to fasting 80-130 mg/dL and postprandial <180 mg/dL)
non_pharmacological: Order definitive localization/staging imaging (contrast-enhanced CT or MRI pancreas/liver; consider somatostatin receptor PET such as 68Ga-DOTATATE if available)
Refer to endocrine surgery/hepatobiliary surgery for evaluation of resection if localized/resectable; multidisciplinary tumor board involvement
Nutrition support: high-protein diet; address weight loss; consider dietitian referral
Assess and treat complications commonly associated with glucagonoma (e.g., venous thromboembolism risk assessment; manage skin findings if present; correct nutritional deficiencies if suspected)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 2-4 weeks to assess symptom control, glucose management, and review imaging/staging; earlier (within 1 week) if severe hyperglycemia or rapid clinical deterioration

must_include: Glucagonoma is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that produces excess glucagon
Excess glucagon can cause high blood sugar/diabetes and weight loss (and may cause characteristic rash and other systemic symptoms)
Confirmatory testing includes fasting glucagon and tumor localization/staging imaging
Somatostatin analog therapy (e.g., octreotide) can reduce glucagon levels and improve symptoms
Blood sugar will be treated and monitored; insulin may be needed initially and adjusted based on readings
Surgery offers the best chance of cure if the tumor is localized and resectable; metastatic disease may require additional systemic options via specialists
Review medication allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify reaction severity before prescribing any new drugs
Discuss expected benefits and common side effects of octreotide (GI upset, gallstones risk, glucose changes) and insulin (hypoglycemia recognition and management)
Provide clear return precautions (severe hypoglycemia, persistent vomiting, dehydration, worsening weakness, or concerning new symptoms)
Home glucose monitoring plan and when to seek help for high/low readings
Dietary guidance to support weight/nutrition and diabetes control (regular meals, adequate protein; avoid skipping meals if on insulin)
Importance of follow-up for imaging results, specialist evaluation, and ongoing lab monitoring

correct_diagnosis.primary: Japanese encephalitis
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with neurologic features consistent with encephalitis (e.g., altered mental status, seizures, focal neurologic deficits) plus exposure risk (travel/residence in endemic area, mosquito exposure, unvaccinated)
CSF pleocytosis (typically lymphocytic) with normal/mildly elevated protein and normal glucose (if CSF obtained)
Japanese encephalitis virus-specific IgM in serum and/or CSF (MAC-ELISA) or confirmatory neutralization testing
Brain MRI compatible with encephalitis (often thalamic/basal ganglia involvement) when available
Exclusion of treatable causes (especially HSV encephalitis and bacterial meningitis) early in course

expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Typically normal (may be low if hyponatremic)

non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; consider ICU for airway protection, seizures, increased intracranial pressure, or hemodynamic instability
Supportive care: IV fluids as needed; avoid hypotonic fluids; correct hyponatremia cautiously
Neurologic monitoring (mental status, seizure precautions); manage raised intracranial pressure if present per protocol

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after starting empiric therapy and stabilization; daily inpatient reassessment with de-escalation once CSF/PCR/serology results return (typically within 24-72 hours); outpatient neurology/infectious disease follow-up 2-4 weeks after discharge for neurocognitive sequelae assessment

Diagnosis requires specific testing (JEV IgM in blood/CSF) and imaging/CSF patterns; early symptoms can be nonspecific (e.g., fatigue) but neurologic symptoms are key red flags
Potential severity and risk of complications (seizures, coma, long-term neurologic deficits)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman who is vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I keep losing my balance and my hearing is getting worse.” Early on, also volunteer: stumbling/clumsy (worse in the dark), muffled hearing/turning TV up, weak hands/dropping things, and occasional numb/tingly feet. Keep timelines imprecise (“a while,” “lately”).

If asked directly, mention intermittent ringing, brief dizziness with quick head turns, bright lights bothering you, trouble on stairs/placing feet, leg cramps/fatigue walking, difficulty hearing in restaurants, and mild slurred speech when tired.

Do NOT volunteer autonomic/sensation issues (not sweating/overheating, not feeling cuts, burns from hot water, constipation, dry gritty eyes) unless specifically asked about sweating, temperature, injuries, bowel habits, or eye dryness.

Resist discussing urinary accidents, sexual function, and social avoidance; minimize or change subject unless asked plainly, then admit reluctantly.

Express misconceptions: you think it might be Ménière’s or MS; blame screen time/“getting older.” Emphasize money/work fears: avoid referrals/genetic testing/hearing devices, want quick OTC fixes. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly and share one extra detail.

correct_diagnosis.primary: PHARC syndrome (ABHD12-related neurodegenerative disorder: Polyneuropathy, Hearing loss, Ataxia, Retinitis pigmentosa, Cataract)
required_evidence: Clinical constellation consistent with PHARC: progressive sensorimotor polyneuropathy, cerebellar ataxia, sensorineural hearing loss, retinitis pigmentosa/visual field loss, and cataracts (often early)
Neuro-ophthalmologic findings consistent with retinal degeneration (e.g., night blindness, peripheral field constriction) with cataract history/exam
Audiology confirming sensorineural hearing loss
Neuropathy confirmed by exam and/or EMG/NCS (length-dependent sensorimotor neuropathy)
Exclusion of common treatable mimics using available labs (e.g., normal/near-normal CK/aldolase and thyroid tests to argue against primary myopathy or hypothyroid myopathy)
Genetic confirmation: biallelic pathogenic variants in ABHD12 (required for definitive diagnosis)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Typically normal or mildly elevated (not in the range expected for primary inflammatory/necrotizing myopathy)
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mild nonspecific elevation; not a primary driver of symptoms
expected_lab_results.Aldolase: Typically normal (supports neuropathic rather than primary myopathic process)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Usually normal (rules out hypothyroidism as a cause of neuropathy/myopathy-like symptoms)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Usually normal unless comorbid diabetes; should not explain the full syndrome
expected_lab_results.Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol): Typically normal (helps exclude vitamin E deficiency/AVED as a treatable mimic with ataxia/neuropathy)

medications: Gabapentin 100-300 mg PO at bedtime, titrate every 3-7 days to effect (e.g., 300 mg PO TID) for neuropathic pain
Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO q6-8h PRN pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) 1-2 drops OU QID PRN dry eye/ocular surface symptoms
Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) 1000-2000 IU PO daily if low bone health risk/limited mobility (adjust to local practice; check level if available)
non_pharmacological: Refer to medical genetics for confirmatory ABHD12 testing and family counseling (autosomal recessive inheritance; offer cascade testing)
Audiology evaluation and hearing rehabilitation (hearing aids; consider cochlear implant candidacy if severe)
Ophthalmology/retina specialist follow-up for retinitis pigmentosa monitoring and cataract management; low-vision services and safety adaptations
Neurology follow-up for neuropathy/ataxia management; consider EMG/NCS if not previously done; fall risk assessment
Physical therapy for gait/ataxia, balance training, strengthening; occupational therapy for ADLs and home safety modifications
Assistive devices as needed (cane/walker), footwear/ankle-foot orthoses if foot drop
Avoid alcohol excess and sedatives that worsen ataxia; review medications that increase fall risk
Vaccination and general preventive care; screen/monitor mood and coping given progressive sensory loss
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, function, and medication tolerability in 4-6 weeks; earlier (1-2 weeks) if rapid titration or significant sedation/falls; ongoing multidisciplinary follow-up every 3-6 months

must_include: PHARC is a rare inherited (autosomal recessive) neurodegenerative condition affecting peripheral nerves, balance/coordination, hearing, and vision (retina) and can include cataracts

Current management is supportive/symptom-directed; early rehabilitation and sensory aids improve safety and function
Discuss prognosis realistically: typically slowly progressive; variability between individuals
Goals: reduce neuropathic pain, improve mobility/balance, optimize hearing and vision support, prevent falls and complications

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT systems administrator. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start by volunteering: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time,” plus frequent nighttime urination ruining sleep, carrying a large water bottle for dry mouth, very clear urine, and feeling more tired/irritable.

If asked for details, admit it’s been months and worsening; if you can’t drink, you feel shaky/panicky and can’t focus; occasional headaches when very thirsty; occasional leakage rushing to the bathroom; a few pounds of unintentional weight loss from drinking more than eating.

Do NOT mention unless specifically and gently asked: craving ice-cold water and chugging, lightheaded standing, dry skin/lips, constipation, avoiding meetings/car rides.

Resist/hedge embarrassment: bedwetting once or twice, drinking at night then rushing to pee, worry coworkers think you’re slacking.

Express misconceptions: fear it’s diabetes; wonder if it’s anxiety, hormones, or migraines. Be skeptical about “optional” tests and costs/time off work; ask about cheaper options. If clinician is empathetic, soften slightly but stay guarded.

Water deprivation test - final urine osm.normal_range: >=600 mOsm/kg expected with intact ADH response

Desmopressin challenge - urine osmolalit.normal_range: Significant rise expected in central DI

medications: Lithium carbonate 300 mg PO twice daily
Lamotrigine 100 mg PO daily
confounders: Chronic lithium exposure (raises suspicion for nephrogenic DI and can coexist with partial central DI)
High caffeine intake (3-4 energy drinks/day) contributing to diuresis
Recent viral upper respiratory infection with reduced oral intake increasing risk of hypernatremic dehydration

differentials: developmental delay, intellectual disability, obesity, and dysmorphic features

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Osmotic diuresis due to diabetes mellitus
required_evidence: Polyuria with 24-hour urine volume >3 L/day (or >50 mL/kg/day) and persistent dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low specific gravity)
Elevated/upper-normal serum sodium and elevated serum osmolality in the setting of polyuria (suggesting free water loss)
Normal fasting serum glucose (to exclude osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia)
Renal function not severely impaired (creatinine not markedly elevated) to support interpretation of urine concentrating ability
Clinical response pattern consistent with central DI (improvement in urine osmolality/volume after desmopressin if assessed)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Often high-normal to elevated (e.g., >145 mmol/L) if water intake does not fully match losses; may be normal if compensatory polydipsia is intact
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Elevated (commonly >295 mOsm/kg) consistent with free water deficit
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute despite elevated serum osmolality (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (typically >3 L/day; may be markedly higher)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (fasting): Normal (not consistent with osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal or mildly elevated from dehydration; disproportionate rise can occur with volume depletion
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal unless concurrent kidney disease; should not explain impaired concentration alone

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily, titrate to lowest effective dose to control polyuria/polydipsia
OR Desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily to twice daily, titrate based on symptoms and sodium
If symptomatic hypernatremia/dehydration: Dextrose 5% in water (D5W) IV, rate individualized with frequent sodium checks (avoid rapid correction)
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water; match intake to thirst unless on scheduled DDAVP (avoid overdrinking when DDAVP active)
Strict intake/output monitoring and daily weights during initiation/titration
Review lithium indication and coordinate with psychiatry to reduce dose or discontinue if feasible; consider alternative mood stabilizer due to DI risk
Patient education on hyponatremia risk with desmopressin (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures) and to hold dose and seek care if symptoms occur
Avoid unnecessary diuretics/NSAIDs unless directed; assess for other causes of polyuria
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and osmolality within 24–48 hours of starting or changing desmopressin, then again at 1–2 weeks; clinical follow-up in 1–2 weeks and after each dose adjustment

must_include: DI is a problem of water balance causing large volumes of dilute urine
Central DI is due to insufficient ADH (vasopressin) action from the brain/pituitary
Lithium can cause nephrogenic DI and must be considered as an alternative/overlapping cause
Explain what the available labs mean: high serum osmolality/sodium with inappropriately dilute urine supports DI
Desmopressin replaces ADH effect and should reduce urine volume and thirst
Use the lowest effective dose; dosing is adjusted based on symptoms and sodium
Main serious risk is water retention and hyponatremia—do not overdrink when DDAVP is working
Need for early and repeated sodium monitoring after starting/changing therapy
Discuss lithium’s role and the plan to coordinate with the prescriber regarding continuation/alternatives
Review red flags requiring urgent care: confusion, severe headache, seizures (hyponatremia) or severe thirst, weakness, reduced intake (hypernatremia/dehydration)
Acknowledge comorbid anxiety/developmental or cognitive issues and ensure caregiver involvement if needed
Maintain access to water and avoid dehydration; track urine output if asked
Avoid excess free-water intake when on desmopressin; drink to thirst unless instructed otherwise
Carry medical information about DI and current medications
Plan for illness/surgery: risk of dehydration or sodium changes; contact clinician for sick-day guidance

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old female administrative assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my days.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights, waking a couple times, daytime tired/foggy, for ~2 months. Only reveal if specifically asked about substances/sleep aids: OTC ‘PM’ a few nights/week (morning grogginess), 1–2 evening drinks to wind down (more awakenings), warm bedroom, lights on while scrolling, less activity/daylight. diagnosis.primary: T-B+ severe combined immunodeficiency due to CD3delta/CD3epsilon/CD3zeta

correct_diagnosis.primary: T−B+ severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) due to CD3 delta/epsilon/zeta complex deficiency (T-cell receptor/CD3 signaling defect)
acceptable_alternatives: T-cell immunodeficiency due to CD3 complex deficiency
Combined immunodeficiency with profound T-cell lymphopenia (SCID-like)
required_evidence: Profound lymphopenia on CBC (very low absolute lymphocyte count for age) consistent with severe T-cell deficiency
Clinical context consistent with SCID risk (severe/recurrent infections, opportunistic infections, failure to thrive, chronic diarrhea, persistent thrush, or severe viral infections)
T−B+ immunophenotype previously established or strongly suspected (absent/very low T cells with preserved B-cell numbers)
No alternative explanation for severe lymphopenia (e.g., recent chemotherapy, high-dose steroids, protein-losing states) in the scenario

expected_lab_results.CBC_WBC: May be low-normal or low; total WBC can be misleadingly normal despite profound lymphopenia
expected_lab_results.CBC_absolute_lymphocyte_count: Markedly decreased for age (severe lymphopenia; often < 1000/µL in infants, can be far lower)
expected_lab_results.CBC_absolute_neutrophil_count: Often normal; may be elevated with bacterial infection or decreased if overwhelmed/sepsis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or mildly low (anemia of chronic disease if ongoing infection/inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Often normal; may be low in sepsis or high with inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: May be elevated if bacterial infection/inflammation present; can be low/variable in viral/opportunistic infections
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: May be elevated with bacterial sepsis; often low in non-bacterial/opportunistic infections
expected_lab_results.Basic_metabolic_panel_sodium: May be low if dehydration/SIADH with infection; otherwise normal

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP component) 5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis)
Fluconazole 6 mg/kg PO daily (fungal prophylaxis, adjust per local protocol and liver function)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg PO QID (HSV/VZV prophylaxis if exposure risk or per immunology protocol)
Immune globulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks (or SCIG equivalent) to maintain protective IgG levels
Cefepime 50 mg/kg IV q8h (empiric broad-spectrum coverage for fever in immunocompromised; modify per cultures/local antibiogram; avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to allergy)
non_pharmacological: Immediate protective isolation and strict infection control (hand hygiene, mask/visitor restrictions); avoid sick contacts

Urgent immunology consultation and expedited definitive therapy planning (hematopoietic stem cell transplantation as curative option; consider gene therapy where available; thymic transplant not applicable to CD3 complex defects)
Avoid live vaccines (e.g., rotavirus, MMR, varicella) and counsel household contacts regarding vaccine strategy (no oral polio; consider inactivated vaccines for contacts)
Use CMV-negative, leukoreduced, irradiated blood products only (prevent transfusion-associated GVHD and CMV transmission)
If febrile or ill: full sepsis evaluation (blood culture, urine culture, chest imaging if respiratory symptoms) and early IV antibiotics; consider antifungal escalation if persistent fever
Nutrition support and monitoring of growth; consider dietitian involvement
Plan for antimicrobial prophylaxis adjustments based on local protocols and organism history
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate same-day immunology/infectious diseases coordination; reassess within 24 hours if febrile/ill (inpatient), otherwise within 1 week for prophylaxis/IVIG initiation and transplant workup timeline

Order confirmatory and baseline evaluation for suspected T−B+ SCID: CBC with differential/ALC, lymphocyte subsets (T/B/NK), immunoglobulins, T-cell function (e.g., mitogen proliferation if available), CMV/EBV testing as clinically indicated, and pre-transplant baseline labs.
Review results to support diagnosis (very low ALC, low/absent T cells with preserved B cells) and assess immediate infection risk/need for urgent escalation.
Make/communicate working diagnosis of T−B+ SCID due to CD3 complex deficiency and document urgency and next steps (infection prevention and transplant referral).
Before prescribing any antimicrobial prophylaxis/treatment, verify reported amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy details and document safe alternatives (critical safety check).
Before prescribing, screen for interactions with planned prophylaxis (e.g., TMP-SMX, azoles) and any current meds/supplements (non-critical but expected).

Before ordering blood products or immunoglobulin-related administration/adjunct orders, perform contraindication check (e.g., IgA deficiency/anaphylaxis history, product requirements) and ensure blood product precautions are specified (critical).

Initiate non-curative infection risk reduction: IVIG replacement and antimicrobial prophylaxis using allergy-appropriate choices; avoid live vaccines; ensure any transfusion orders specify irradiated/CMV-negative/leukoreduced products.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/urgency, explain treatment (prevention vs cure), address allergy and warning signs, and provide lifestyle/exposure and household vaccination guidance.
Arrange urgent immunology/hematology referral for hematopoietic stem cell transplantation evaluation; schedule IVIG/prophylaxis monitoring and clear escalation plan.
Close encounter after safety-netting, confirming understanding, and documenting urgent follow-up.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia complaint details (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), duration/frequency (short-term vs chronic), severity, daytime impairment, and patient goals/concerns.

Screen for contributing factors and differentials: sleep schedule, naps, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine, exercise timing, stress/anxiety/depression, pain, reflux, nocturia, medications/substances, and prior treatments.

Assess red flags/secondary sleep disorders: OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), restless legs/PLMD (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night), parasomnias, severe daytime sleepiness/near-miss accidents, mania, suicidality.
Order targeted labs only to evaluate contributing conditions when indicated (e.g., TSH, CBC, ferritin/iron studies) and explicitly not as a confirmatory test for insomnia.
Review lab results to identify/exclude contributory medical conditions (thyroid disease, anemia/iron deficiency) that may worsen sleep.
Make and explain the diagnosis (insomnia disorder vs short-term insomnia), document duration/frequency criteria, and note perpetuating factors.

Provide first-line treatment counseling: CBT-I (stimulus control, sleep restriction/time-in-bed consolidation, cognitive strategies), sleep diary, expectations over weeks, and lifestyle guidance (caffeine/alcohol timing).
Critical safety check before any prescribing: confirm medication allergies and reaction types.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: evaluate interactions with current meds/substances (including alcohol, OTC antihistamines, cannabis, opioids, benzodiazepines).
Clarify contraindications and risk factors relevant to sedative-hypnotics (pregnancy, OSA risk, severe COPD, hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, fall risk, driving/work safety).

If medication is chosen via shared decision-making, prescribe an appropriate short-course/lowest-effective-dose option with clear duration, avoidance of Carbromal, and reassessment plan; otherwise skip prescribing.
If any sedating medication is started, counsel on safe use: avoid alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment, and caution with driving/operating machinery; reinforce CBT-I and sleep diary.
Arrange follow-up (typically 2–4 weeks) to review sleep diary, response to CBT-I/meds, adverse effects, and to taper/stop meds if used; provide earlier return precautions for red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared plan, and safety-net instructions.

Start by saying your gums started bleeding a lot after brushing this morning and it’s been hard to stop. Early in the visit, volunteer that you have hemophilia and you’re scared this is serious. Clinical context consistent with postpartum pituitary infarction/hemorrhage (e.g., history of severe postpartum hemorrhage, failure to lactate, amenorrhea—if available)

expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately high (not maximally dilute) despite low serum osmolality

non_pharmacological: If symptomatic hyponatremia or suspected adrenal crisis: immediate IV 0.9% saline; monitor sodium correction rate
Provide sick-day rules and emergency steroid injection education; advise medical alert identification
Endocrinology referral for full pituitary hormone evaluation and long-term management
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks after starting glucocorticoids and/or thyroid hormone; sooner (24-72 hours) if hospitalized or symptomatic hyponatremia/adrenal crisis

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit postpartum history (severe hemorrhage/hypotension), lactation failure, amenorrhea, fatigue, weight change, dizziness/syncope, nausea/vomiting, cold intolerance, and medication history to raise suspicion for hypopituitarism/secondary adrenal insufficiency.

Screen for adrenal crisis red flags (persistent vomiting, severe weakness, confusion, severe abdominal pain, hypotension), intercurrent illness, and ability to take oral meds to determine urgency and need for emergency treatment.

Order initial evaluation for suspected Sheehan syndrome: 8 AM cortisol and ACTH, BMP (Na/K/glucose), free T4 and TSH, prolactin, LH/FSH with estradiol, IGF-1; consider pregnancy test if relevant; consider pituitary MRI after stabilization.

Interpret labs for central adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH), central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with low/normal TSH), and other pituitary deficits; identify hyponatremia/hypoglycemia requiring prompt management.
Make the diagnosis of Sheehan syndrome with hypopituitarism and prioritize secondary adrenal insufficiency as immediately life-threatening.
Confirm and document allergies (including penicillin urticarial rash) before prescribing; clarify that planned steroid/thyroid therapy is unrelated.
Check for interactions affecting glucocorticoids/levothyroxine (e.g., enzyme inducers, anticoagulants, diabetes meds, iron/calcium, bile acid sequestrants) before prescribing.

Start glucocorticoid replacement first (e.g., hydrocortisone split dosing; consider stress-dose or parenteral steroids if unstable/actively vomiting). Avoid starting thyroid hormone until steroids are initiated to prevent precipitating adrenal crisis.

Explain diagnosis and risks (low cortisol dangers; misleading TSH in central hypothyroidism), provide hydrocortisone administration instructions, sick-day rules, emergency injection use, red flags, hydration guidance for hyponatremia, medical alert, and emergency kit planning.
After steroid replacement is established and patient is stable, add/titrate levothyroxine guided by free T4 (not TSH). Hold liothyronine initially as it is not first-line here.

Arrange close follow-up (days to 1–2 weeks initially) with repeat electrolytes/glucose/free T4, symptom review, dose adjustment, and endocrinology referral; plan pituitary imaging if not already done.
Close the encounter after ensuring safety netting and follow-up are in place.

Non-cardiac differentials (pulmonary hypertension and pneumonias) not supported by available data/labs (no infectious/inflammatory pulmonary syndrome provided; troponin pattern not consistent with primary pneumonia)

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly distinguishes stable CAD symptoms from possible acute coronary syndrome by eliciting chest pain features (exertional vs rest, duration, progression) and, when available, referencing troponin trend; if ACS concern exists, agent escalates urgency rather than proceeding as routine outpatient management.
Agent explains CAD pathophysiology as plaque buildup in coronary arteries and links autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia to very high lifelong LDL and increased early CAD risk.

Agent uses the lab pattern (very high LDL) to support FH and explains why lung/infectious differentials are less likely given the available information, without dismissing them if symptoms suggest otherwise.
Before prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check that includes aspirin and penicillin and captures reaction types; agent avoids aspirin due to prior urticaria/bronchospasm.

Before prescribing, agent checks for contraindications to statin therapy (pregnancy, active liver disease/significant transaminitis) and addresses them appropriately; statins are not prescribed in pregnancy.
Agent performs a drug interaction check (at minimum for statin interactions) and adjusts statin choice/dose when interacting drugs are present.

Treatment explanation includes: LDL-lowering goal to reduce MI/stroke risk; high-intensity statin as first-line; escalation to ezetimibe and/or PCSK9 inhibitor if LDL goals not met or intolerance occurs; and when antiplatelet therapy is indicated, discusses clopidogrel as an aspirin alternative.

Agent counsels on expected benefits, common adverse effects (statin myalgias, liver enzyme elevations; PCSK9 injection-site reactions), and provides clear instructions on what to do if adverse effects occur (e.g., stop and contact clinician for severe symptoms, evaluate CK/LFTs as appropriate).
Agent includes shared decision-making on medication intensity and add-on therapy based on LDL level, ASCVD risk, and patient preferences/constraints (cost, injections, adherence).

Lifestyle counseling includes LDL-specific dietary guidance (reduce saturated fat; increase soluble fiber/plant sterols), an exercise plan tailored to tolerance and symptoms, and smoking cessation if relevant.
Agent emphasizes family screening/cascade testing due to autosomal dominant inheritance and suggests evaluation of first-degree relatives.
Agent provides red-flag symptoms requiring urgent evaluation: new/worsening chest pain, dyspnea at rest, syncope (and similar concerning symptoms).
Agent schedules follow-up lipid testing (4–12 weeks after initiation or dose change) and ongoing monitoring, and reinforces adherence.

minimal_paths.description: Stable CAD risk management in suspected/known autosomal dominant FH with very high LDL; aspirin allergy present; outpatient optimization with aggressive lipid lowering and appropriate antiplatelet alternative when indicated.

PRESCRIBE high-intensity statin; plan add ezetimibe/PCSK9 if goals unmet; avoid aspirin and discuss clopidogrel when antiplatelet indicated

SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP lipids in 4–12 weeks and ongoing monitoring
